title: EoTV EoE Narrative Concurrency Reasons for and Against
display_title: EoTV EoE Narrative Concurrency Reasons for and Against
wikitext:
[]: EoTV/EoE Narrative Concurrency: Reasons for and Against             Quote Post by M0arDakka » Wed Oct 10, 2012 3:40 am So while I was browsing /a/, I came upon an Eva thread, and naturally I investigated. One thing that caught my eye among the trolls, fanboyism and cynical assholes was the recurring idea that episodes 25 and 26 exist concurrently with the End of Evangelion, which consists of episodes 25' and 26'. I saw that this belief appeared to have broad support among posters, and personally I had problems reconciling the two endings after finishing the series, and following a good bit of thought I decided that EoE and EoTV are incompatible; in short, I see EoTV as a 'test run' of the show's ending, a result of budget and time constraints, and the rejection of the original script of episode 25 (which I think was reused as the basis of 25'), and EoE as a more finished product, complete with the benefits of a larger budget and a theatrical release. I present my first piece of evidence: Shinji witnessing the sex scene between Misato and Kaji. In episode 25, Shinji is shown part of Misato's college sex marathon with Kaji, and Misato reacts in a mortified manner, claiming to be ashamed of that aspect of herself, while part of her says she was glad to share it with Kaji: (skip to 5:03, apologies if you do not like the dub, it is what I could find) [url]http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-BFzSxTql0w&feature=relmfu[/url] However, in 26', Shinji is shown the same scene, but this time he reacts with shock, and Misato simply states that this is another part of herself, one that must be accepted, like all of reality: (skip to 1:00:50, also apologies in advance for dub) http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jMjnvZhYtrM While Shinji has an angry, almost betrayed look on his face in both incarnations, Misato's differing reactions to his knowledge of her intimate nature creates too much dissonance between the two versions for them to be the same. In addition, the fact that if EoTV was to be inserted, say, following the breakup lines at the end of Komm, Susser Tod, it would simply not make narrative sense for the same scene to be repeated twice with different results (kind of like film insanity)! This is my first point. I await critiques and possibly additional support or even opposition. I'm intent on hearing your answers.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Wed Oct 10, 2012 3:57 am Background reading http://forum.evageeks.org/thread/8122/ http://forum.evageeks.org/thread/12277/ http://forum.evageeks.org/thread/10494/ http://forum.evageeks.org/thread/8141/

[]:             Quote Post by M0arDakka » Wed Oct 10, 2012 4:37 am Thanks. Should I bring my argument to one of these threads, or is my own thread still appropriate? Edit: Oh shit, there's a LOT of threads like this. I'm feeling like I should choose the least dead one.

[]:             Quote Post by Blue Monday » Wed Oct 10, 2012 5:31 am A point of interest for OP; Concurrency doesn't necessarily mean that EOTV and EOE go perfectly hand-in-hand. Just that certain themes, sequences, ideas and narrative points in the story complement/support each other. I'm too lazy to write further, there are a great plethora of examples though. Might come back later.

[]: Concurrent action. B/             Quote Post by Alaska Slim » Wed Oct 10, 2012 6:43 am ^Depends on whether you mean strong or weak concurrency. Just for lulz, there's also this bit of lunacy.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Wed Oct 10, 2012 11:20 am M0arDakka wrote: Edit: Oh shit, there's a LOT of threads like this.

[]:             Quote Post by thewayneiac » Wed Oct 10, 2012 12:06 pm Blue Monday wrote: A point of interest for OP; Concurrency doesn't necessarily mean that EOTV and EOE go perfectly hand-in-hand. Just that certain themes, sequences, ideas and narrative points in the story complement/support each other.

[]:             Quote Post by M0arDakka » Wed Oct 10, 2012 3:19 pm Well, seems that this topic still holds interest, so I'll continue with my argument. @thewayniac: True, Misato does state in both that Shinji one way or another should be aware of this part of her. The initial objection, however in EoTV is absent from the EoE version, along with Kaji's response. Also, while ultimately Misato may want Shinji to know, he sees her college experience during Pre-Instrumentality in EoE, whereas in EoTV this occurs during Instrumentality proper. I'll continue later today if possible, lunch is only so long.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Wed Oct 10, 2012 4:43 pm I edited my post above to add names to each theory as a tool to encourage posters to clearly articulate which version they're arguing for or against. Without that this can quickly turn into a clusterfuck. And for the new folks who don't bother to read all those links Tine's posted, I favor Strong Split [SS].

[]:             Quote Post by M0arDakka » Wed Oct 10, 2012 7:31 pm The responses I've seen highlight the need for a definition of what I want to argue about. To put it clearly, the type of concurrency I'm concerned with is narrative concurrency, the idea that "episodes 25 and 26 take place in the minds of the characters during End of Evangelion". This assertion bothers me not because 'based on my point of view, it's wrong', but rather that it suggests that the two endings are directly connected by storyline. So when a viewer sees both, whether one after the other or spliced together to suit their viewing, he or she automatically assumes that they must fit together in the same timeline, either sequentially or in an overlapping manner. Since I've noticed that some people become utterly confused by trying to piece them together, and they abandon the show altogether, possibly going on to badmouth it in the future. I wish to see if 1) if the two endings exist within the same narrative, and 2) if so, how are they concurrent. @NemZ: I really appreciate your efforts to delineate the views on concurrency, I am particularly interested in the SS theory, since I took that the DC versions of 21-24 replaced the on-air versions, and saw the additional content as expository in preparation for EoE rather than any sort of retcon. If anyone would provide examples of the latter notion, I could work from there. Fundamentally, I wish to derive the most accurate explanation concerning the endings, regardless of the success of my personal argument. Although for me Evangelion is all about the emotions, it's nice when it makes sense as well. Edit: Changed the title to focus the subject of the thread.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Wed Oct 10, 2012 8:17 pm M0arDakka wrote: I am particularly interested in the SS theory, since I took that the DC versions of 21-24 replaced the on-air versions, and saw the additional content as expository in preparation for EoE rather than any sort of retcon.

[]:             Quote Post by Hyper Shinchan » Wed Oct 10, 2012 8:33 pm NemZ wrote: The additional material puts a very different spin on some of the character relationships towards the end, and thus sets up a different ending scenario.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Wed Oct 10, 2012 9:11 pm Granted most of the changes are just visual/sound fixes and cleanup, but there's actually several entire scenes that weren't in the OA episodes at all. I think the Wiki has a detailed breakdown, but just off the top of my head some of the most important additions include: 21 - The Katsuragi second impact recording is all new, including the Gendo/Kiel conversation deriding scientists. Fuyu/Yui scenes indicating her being trapped in the core was an intentional part of her plan. 22 - Asuka's inept flirting with Kaji on the aircraft carrier, being jealous over Shinji talking to Rei, her hatefest in the bathroom, and most of Asuka's mind rape scenes including the part that indicates she wasn't just screwing around in the kiss and the 'Wall of Jericho' scenes much earlier. 23 - Most of the fight with Armisael, including all the parts where angel/Rei is reaching out to Shinji and bonding to him as well as the angel tower and the first giant naked Rei. This changes theconnotations of her choice to self destruct from being less about killing the angel and more about protecting Shinji. 24 - Kaworu's conversation with the 16 monoliths that we still have yet to come to a consensus on. There's dozens of slight changes to expressions or extra/removed sentences throughout the 4 as well, but it's harder to remember all of those.

[]:             Quote Post by M0arDakka » Wed Oct 10, 2012 9:18 pm Once again, definition strikes again! God I should read my own quotes. @NemZ: I concede that the DC edits change aspects of the show, especially character relationships like that of Shinji and Asuka's, but the episodes themselves do not (I think) contradict their on-air counterparts with the new information. @Hyper Shinchan: One example of an important inclusion in the DC versions is in episode 22'; the mind-rape sequence has Asuka shouting at an image of Shinji about how he isn't/wasn't there for her. This makes Asuka's relationship to Shinji more explicit, setting the stage for their confrontation in EoE. Edit: NemZ you beat me to the punch, but I hope my two cents shed some more light on the subject.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Wed Oct 10, 2012 9:30 pm M0arDakka wrote: @NemZ: I concede that the DC edits change aspects of the show, especially character relationships like that of Shinji and Asuka's, but the episodes themselves do not (I think) contradict their on-air counterparts with the new information.

[]:             Quote Post by M0arDakka » Wed Oct 10, 2012 9:35 pm In my opinion every other approach is either trying far too hard to make two round pegs fit in one square hole or are simply denying half the show exists.

[]:             Quote Post by Hyper Shinchan » Wed Oct 10, 2012 9:41 pm Thanks NemZ and M0arDakka, I especially forgot the differences in the mindraping scene. M0arDakka wrote: I'll make sure to split it into a two-part ending: first eps. 21-26, then 21'-24' and EoE.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Thu Oct 11, 2012 1:39 am @NemZ 24' also added the kitchen/coffee-pot scene at the beginning, which makes viewing EoE after the on-air series more WTF!? than was intended -- such a pivotal scene referring back to something that must have happened in the objective world; but what exactly?

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Thu Oct 11, 2012 2:25 am True, forgot about that one. There's just so much they added it gets hard to keep them straight sometimes! Worth pointing out for anyone who might not be aware is that most of the DC additions were originally part of the 'recap' half of the first theatrical release, so there is some expectation that the audience will know what it's referring too. This also makes it even more obvious that a retcon is in effect when those scenes get blended into the existing episodes (along with more changes that were made even later (most of the the changes I mentioned in 23, for example), when EoE had already been released) and thus provides a very clear dividing line for splitting the plot. To my mind should invalidate the [WS] viewpoint... if you split it at the movie then the DC eps which were part of the movie should naturally be split as well, thus [SS]. To be honest I only see [SS] and [WC] as being remotely feasible because [SC] has contradictions and pointless repetitions to reconcile while [RN] is just [SS] for the lazy and/or closed-minded. I reject [WC] because it's trying to have it's cake and eat it too (ending up with a sum that's less then it's parts), but at least it isn't self-defeating, just misguided.

[]:             Quote Post by Blue Monday » Thu Oct 11, 2012 5:41 am NemZ wrote: "Weak Concurrency (WC)" - EoE and EoTV both happen, but EoE remixes some previous scenes and takes precedence wherever there is a conflict. "Strong Concurrency (SC)" - EoE and EoTV both happened exactly as shown, with the true chronology skipping back and forth between them.
